primary_diagnosis.name: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (likely AVNRT given prominent neck pounding/cannon a-waves), currently sustained and haemodynamically stable

pathophysiology_summary: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is a re-entrant tachyarrhythmia producing abrupt-onset, regular, rapid rates (often 150-220 bpm). In a young/middle-aged patient with a structurally normal heart, the common mechanism is atrioventricular nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) or an accessory-pathway-mediated re-entry; the prominent pounding in the neck with visible rapid venous pulsations (cannon a-waves) reflects atrial contraction against a closed tricuspid valve and is characteristic of AVNRT. The abrupt onset/offset ('switch flipped') and perfect regularity distinguish SVT from sinus tachycardia (gradual, rate proportionate to stimulus) and atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular); a stable patient conversing at 185 bpm with a normal blood pressure and structurally normal heart makes ventricular tachycardia less likely, though a broad-complex tachycardia would change this. Heavy caffeine lowers the arrhythmia threshold. While SVT in a structurally normal heart is usually not immediately life-threatening, a sustained fast rate causes presyncope, and prolonged tachycardia risks fatigue/decompensation; haemodynamic instability (hypotension, severe chest pain, syncope, pulmonary oedema) would mandate emergency care including synchronised cardioversion. First-line termination is a vagal manoeuvre (a modified Valsalva is most effective); if unsuccessful, pharmacological termination with intravenous adenosine under ECG monitoring is used, so a 12-lead ECG capturing the rhythm in tachycardia and monitored treatment are needed. These require in-person assessment; a vagal manoeuvre can be safely coached over telehealth, but adenosine/cardioversion cannot be given remotely, so urgent in-person assessment is required, escalating to emergency if unstable.

differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt-onset ('switch flipped'), regular, very fast palpitations at rest with neck pounding are classic for SVT.
Anxiety/caffeine can raise heart rate, but the abrupt onset and a rate of ~185 at rest are out of proportion and more consistent with SVT.
Considered for palpitations, but a perfectly regular rhythm argues against the irregularly irregular pulse of AF.

Must be kept in mind for any sustained tachycardia, though a young structurally-normal, stable, conversing patient with a normal blood pressure favours SVT; a broad-complex tachycardia on ECG would change this.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise probable SVT, assess haemodynamic stability, and coach a vagal manoeuvre while arranging urgent ECG capture.

differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt regular ~185 bpm tachycardia (DI-001) sustained ~45 min (DI-002) with presyncope and neck pounding (DI-003) but no crushing chest pain/syncope (DI-004) in a caffeine-exposed patient indicates stable SVT needing a vagal manoeuvre then urgent ECG/adenosine.
Abrupt onset and a disproportionate rate at rest are not sinus tachycardia.
A perfectly regular rhythm excludes AF.
Remains a consideration until the ECG confirms a narrow-complex tachycardia; haemodynamic stability and a structurally normal heart favour SVT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is stable SVT: coach a vagal manoeuvre; if unsuccessful, arrange urgent in-person ECG and adenosine, escalating to emergency if unstable.

differential.diagnosis: Paroxysmal SVT (suspected AVNRT) - requiring 12-lead ECG in tachycardia and monitored termination
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Definitive rhythm diagnosis needs a 12-lead ECG captured during tachycardia; vagal manoeuvres can be coached, but adenosine/cardioversion require in-person monitored care.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is stable SVT - coach a vagal manoeuvre, arrange urgent in-person ECG/adenosine if it persists, and escalate to 000 if unstable.

red_flags.description: Abrupt-onset, regular, very rapid tachycardia (~185 bpm) at rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates a paroxysmal re-entrant SVT requiring rhythm capture and termination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated sustained tachycardia causes ongoing presyncope and risks decompensation.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A vagal-refractory, sustained episode needs prompt medical termination (ECG-guided adenosine) if a vagal manoeuvre fails.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prolonged tachycardia leads to fatigue and possible haemodynamic decline.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic-instability features (crushing chest pain, syncope, hypotension) - currently ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: If any develop, the SVT is unstable and requires emergency care (including synchronised cardioversion); their absence supports stable management with a vagal manoeuvre and urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If instability develops and is not acted on, cardiovascular collapse can occur.

red_flags.description: Narrow-complex regular tachycardia on 12-lead ECG captured in tachycardia, defining the SVT mechanism
red_flags.clinical_significance: The 12-lead ECG (a regular narrow-complex tachycardia, and post-conversion rhythm) defines the arrhythmia mechanism and cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires a 12-lead ECG; the correct action is urgent in-person rhythm capture.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anxiety and caffeine can contribute, but an abrupt regular ~185 bpm at rest is SVT, not simple sinus tachycardia, and needs rhythm capture.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to coach a vagal manoeuvre
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A modified Valsalva is a safe, effective first-line telehealth-coachable intervention for stable SVT that is often missed.
The AI Doctor must screen for crushing chest pain, syncope and hypotension, which would upgrade this to an emergency needing cardioversion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Advising the patient to drive himself
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Driving while tachycardic and near-fainting on standing risks collapse at the wheel; assisted transport or ambulance is safer.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG capturing the rhythm in tachycardia (narrow-complex regular tachycardia) and post-conversion tracing
Accurate clinician-measured heart rate and blood pressure during the episode
Cardiovascular examination findings (regular rapid pulse, cannon a-waves, absence of murmurs/gallops/rubs)
Administration of intravenous adenosine or synchronised cardioversion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Coach a safe vagal manoeuvre (modified Valsalva) first; if the SVT persists, arrange urgent in-person assessment for a 12-lead ECG in tachycardia and monitored pharmacological termination (adenosine); advise assisted transport (not self-driving), and call 000 if severe/crushing chest pain, worsening breathlessness, or syncope develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing a sustained 185 bpm regular tachycardia to anxiety/caffeine and advising the patient to wait, or to drive himself, risks prolonged tachycardia, presyncope and collapse at the wheel, and misses an ECG rhythm capture.
